does: This paper presents TITA, a token-level inference-time alignment framework for vision-language models that addresses hallucination and output misalignment without retraining the base model. The approach freezes the VLM and trains a lightweight reward model to approximate its output distribution. During generation, implicit preference signals are extracted as log-probability ratios between the reward model and the target VLM, providing dense autoregressive feedback at each token. The method functions as an inference-time variant of Direct Preference Optimization, delivering token-level corrective signals that guide decoding. Evaluations on LLaVA-1.5 (7B and 13B variants) across 12 benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements, with gains of 8.6% on MMVet and 6.7% on POPE, showing stronger performance in both factual grounding and hallucination reduction without architectural modifications to the base model.
connects: This work builds on recent research addressing VLM hallucination and output alignment, extending beyond existing sequence-level inference strategies like beam search or constrained decoding that provide only coarse feedback. It connects to the preference-based alignment literature, particularly Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), which showed that fine-tuning with preference pairs avoids expensive reinforcement learning. The paper responds to limitations of standard fine-tuning approaches that require large annotated preference datasets and modification of model weights. It also engages with token-level decoding strategies and reward-guided generation methods from the broader language model literature, adapting these techniques to the multimodal setting where visual grounding is essential for reducing hallucination.
enables: This inference-time alignment approach opens several research directions for VLMs and multimodal systems. The lightweight reward model training suggests scalable alignment methods that could be applied to larger models without fine-tuning overhead, potentially enabling rapid adaptation to new domains or safety requirements. The token-level feedback mechanism could be extended to other multimodal tasks beyond vision-language generation, such as visual reasoning or embodied AI applications. Future work might explore combining this approach with other inference-time techniques for improved diversity-accuracy tradeoffs, scaling to higher-resolution visual inputs, or developing task-specific reward models. The method also creates opportunities for interactive alignment where user feedback could update the reward model between requests, enabling personalized VLM behavior without retraining.
